url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qNZW6uCuvDs
title: The Macro Minute: Where are we in the global liquidity cycle?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with
another macro minute for Thursday, May
14th, 2026. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
Lead-Off Morning Note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
where are we in the global liquidity
cycle? Our answer is the contraction in
the PBOC's balance sheet is weighing on
Chinese liquidity, which is now up only
12% on a 3-month annualized basis versus
up 27% in February. Recall that China
and the US are home to the two deepest
pools of liquidity in the world. The
positive impulses in Chinese and US
liquidity are supporting a continued
expansion of global liquidity, which is
up 3% on a month-over-month annualized
basis and up 5% on a 3-month annualized
basis.
Global liquidity has been expanding on a
trend basis every month since the start
of 2025. A Fed catch-up trade or an
extended closure of the Strait of Hormuz
would be supportive of the US dollar and
currency volatility and hawkish for
interest rates in support of a bond
volatility. This outcome would be
negative for global liquidity and risk
assets over the short to medium-term.
Refer to yesterday's Lead-Off Morning
Note for May 13th for more details. As
always, I'll wrap up with a question
from our community here in our 42 macro
dashboard. This one's titled AI CapEx
bubble. It says, "Hi, Darius. Thanks for
all that you do for the community. My
portfolio has outperformed benchmarks by
a significant margin with the help of
KISS and Dr. Mo." Thank you for the kind
feedback. Uh getting back to the
question, it says, "I'm having trouble
reconciling two long-term fundamental
calls that you have made regarding AI.
Number one, AI CapEx build-out will end
in a bubble that bursts just like all
the historical massive infrastructure
investment cycles like the dot-com
bubble or roads, etc. versus number two,
AI is already so advanced that it can
already replace a broad swath of high
very high-value white-collar jobs
ranging from banking, finance,
consulting, engineering, etc.
Opportunity to capture extra profits
from removing expensive labor makes this
highly likely, if not inevitable." So,
my key takeaway is that I don't I I
think the the concept that these are two
diametrically opposed
themes is is is is inaccurate in my
opinion. The reason I say that is that
because because number two exists,
because AI is so advanced and is likely
to create tremendous gains from a
macroeconomic standpoint with respect to
productivity and profitability, because
that is true, the that is why we are
having an AI CapEx
bubble. You know, it's almost like you
know, I'm trying to use an analogy. If
you put a briefcase with $10 million
in the middle of a crowded busy highway,
most people will still try to go pick up
the briefcase with the
with $10 million in it. If the if the
rewards are so obvious, then they're
willing to
you know, tremendous you know, take on a
tremendous amount of risk to capture
those rewards. And as a function of
that,
you know, that that that you know, that
emotional response to the obvious prize
that is AI, you're ultimately going to
have an overbuilding
uh uh that you hit you you traditionally
seen this, you know, they're going to
eventually overbuild. Who knows when
that we they will overbuild, but
certainly it's highly likely that they
will overbuild, which is exactly what we
saw in the railroad CapEx bubble, which
is exactly what we saw in the consumer
durable goods CapEx bubble, which is
exactly what we saw in the information
technology CapEx bubble in the late
'90s, early 2000s. And so obviously
internet technology changed the world. I
wouldn't be talking to you over the
internet if that did not if that wasn't
true. But again, the Nasdaq still went
down 90% from its peak. I want to say in
March of 2000. So you have to separate
the impact of the technology from the
CapEx cycle itself and ultimately from
the market cycle and the market
response. And so I'll just kind of wrap
up, just kind of look at a chart of the
Nasdaq 100 here.
This is our this is our Dr. Mo signal
for the Nasdaq 100. Dr. Mo got us back
long the Nasdaq 100 back on April, I
think it was on April 11th here. And so
you know, it's just been a wicked
straight line up. There's a lot of
crowded bullish positioning in this
trade. We're not making the call that
this is the beginning of the secular
bear market that we expect to follow the
AI CapEx bubble, but you have to assume
that if we continue to see dynamics like
this in the marketplace and an
increasing crowding into this theme and
an increasing concentration in asset
markets, soon enough the entire world
and all of the investors in it will have
one position on and there'll be nobody
on the other side of the trade when they
decide to start taking profits. And that
is the key risk. So, we'll wrap it up
there. Darius Dale here presenting our
macro minute for Thursday, May 14th,
2026. Best of luck out there today.
We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
